text: The video shows numerous adhesions of the small bowel to the diseased area, which are visibly prominent.
The surgeon addresses unusual congenital attachments between the terminal ileum mesentery and the sigmoid colon, preparing to manage these connections.
The robotic surgical system is docked, positioning its arms for the upcoming sigmoid resection procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to manipulate tissues in an effort to restore normal anatomical structures in the area affected by diverticulitis.
The surgeon carefully separates the small bowel from the colon, addressing adhesions in the area of disease.
The surgeon carefully manipulates a loop of ileum that is fused to the medial aspect of the proximal sigmoid colon, using gentle back-and-forth movements to separate the structures.
The surgeon carefully searches for natural tissue planes between the small bowel and colon, avoiding the creation of artificial planes during dissection.
The surgeon observes unusual folded attachments between the sigmoid mesentery and the terminal ileum mesentery, highlighting the complexity of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully separates a loop of small bowel from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery to improve visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon successfully separates the loop of small bowel from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon observes the attachment of the terminal ileum (TI) mesentery to the sigmoid colon, highlighting the anatomical connection between these structures.
The surgeon observes a congenital attachment between the sigmoid mesentery and the surrounding structures.
The surgeon attempts to access the area behind the mesosigmoid, approaching the sacral promontory.
The surgeon observes the sigmoid colon adhered to the right pelvic side wall, indicating a need for careful dissection to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon observes congenital attachments of the sigmoid colon, distinguishing them from those typically caused by diverticular disease.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the bowel, preparing to resect the section that is adhered to the right pelvic side wall.
The surgeon navigates into the true pelvis, advancing the dissection deeper into the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon carefully separates the sigmoid colon from the sidewall, ensuring clear visualization of the ureter and mesentery.
The surgeon navigates posteriorly, carefully avoiding the presacral plexus while continuing the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, ensuring its location is clear during the dissection process in the pelvic region.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter as it crosses over the psoas muscle, highlighting its anatomical position during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully navigates within the mesorectal plane, positioned posterior to the rectal mesentery, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The ureter is visible along the right aspect of the rectum, positioned more medially than typical, highlighting its proximity to the rectal mesentery.
The surgeon navigates the retrorectal space, known as the holy plane, maintaining dissection in the mesorectal plane as typically done during a total mesorectal excision (TME) for rectal cancer.
The surgeon has freed the sigmoid colon, allowing it to move into the pelvic cavity, completing the dissection in the retrorectal space.
The surgeon examines the mesentery, focusing on understanding its orientation and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon observes a thickened fold of mesentery leading towards the root of the superior hemorrhoidal artery and the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), which is atypical in appearance.
The surgeon carefully navigates within the retrorectal space, maintaining dissection within the known anatomical plane.
The left common iliac artery is visible in the surgical field, identifiable by its location and anatomical structure.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field, searching for the left ureter within the mesentery.
The surgeon lifts the mesentery off the retroperitoneum, aiming to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal artery while ensuring minimal risk of injury to the ureter or gonadal vessels.
The surgeon unfolds the remaining mesentery, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon transitions from medial to lateral dissection, focusing on the lateral dissection of the mesentery.
The surgeon marks the anticipated proximal resection margin on the colon, ensuring it remains identifiable during the dissection process.
The left ureter is visually identified and exposed in the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, detaching it from surrounding structures to facilitate the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates movement along the gonadal vessels, highlighting how their appearance can mimic that of the ureter.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissues to achieve the necessary exposure.
The surgeon has placed four 8-millimeter ports for the laparoscopic colectomy procedure.
The surgical field is maintained in the original configuration with the patient in steep Trendelenburg position, left side elevated.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure, utilizing the visibility and safety provided by the current positioning.
The spleen is visible in the surgical field during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas to lift the mesentery of the sigmoid colon away from it, ensuring clear separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon returns to the area near the kidney, where the pancreas begins to become visible.
The surgeon works to separate the mesentery of the splenic flexure from the pancreas, using careful dissection techniques to achieve this anatomical separation.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery of the sigmoid colon away from the pancreas, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a previously created window above the superior hemorrhoidal vessels to manipulate the mesentery.
The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery towards the desired proximal margin, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon indicates the location for the distal margin of the resection.
The surgeon uses an energy device to take additional bites through the mesentery, progressively dividing the tissue.
The surgeon cleans the upper rectum, removing any residual tissue or debris to ensure a clear surgical field.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the bowel, likely using visual cues such as color and pulsation to assess blood flow in the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the proximal margin of the colon for resection, marking the area where the surgical cut will be made.
The surgeon identifies the distal margin of the colon, preparing to make a precise cut for resection.
Under white light, the surgeon divides the tissue at the proximal margin using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery on the upper rectum, applying controlled energy to the tissue.
The surgeon performs the distal division of the colon, using surgical instruments to carefully cut along the predetermined distal margin.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum to a lower position in preparation for a transrectal extraction.
The surgeon chops the specimen into smaller pieces, preparing it for transrectal extraction.
The surgeon uses a ring clamp to individually extract specimens from below, demonstrating the technique for transrectal extraction.
The surgeon cuts another piece of the specimen, using precise techniques to divide the tissue into smaller sections.
The surgeon uses a ring clamp to extract specimens from below, continuing the process of specimen removal.
The surgeon uses a ring clamp to extract another piece of tissue from the surgical site.
The surgeon cuts the final piece of tissue during the colectomy procedure, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon performs a sewn end-to-end anastomosis, avoiding the use of linear and circular staplers, while utilizing eight millimeter trocars.
The surgeon completes the back row of sutures using 2-0 PDS and proceeds to apply an inner layer of sutures using 3-0 V-Loc.
The surgeon demonstrates the suture technique from the opposite side, threading the suture around and over the top to invert the common anastomosis.
The surgeon continues suturing back and forth to bring the two suture lines together, aiming to meet in the middle of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon completes the inner layer of the anastomosis using V-lock sutures, finalizing the closure of the bowel.
The surgeon completes the second outer anterior row of sutures using two OPDS, securing the anastomosis with visible precision.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using two layers of absorbable sutures, ensuring the bowel lumen remains wide open and accommodates well.
The surgeon completes the suture by bringing it back around to the original posterior row, ensuring the continuity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to perform a leak test on the bowel anastomosis to ensure there are no leaks in the sutured area.
The surgeon confirms the absence of any leakage following the completion of the leak test on the bowel anastomosis.
